text: My grandmother's kitchen was a modest space — barely enough room for two people to stand side by side.
Yet everything that mattered in our family seemed to originate there.
She would rise before dawn, and the aroma of fresh bread would linger throughout the house by the time we woke.
Her recipes were never written down.
She relied on instinct and decades of practice, adjusting flavours with a swift confidence I have never witnessed in anyone else.
When I was an adolescent, I dismissed her cooking as ordinary.
It was only after she passed away that I grasped how remarkable those meals truly were.
Each dish carried traces of her heritage and the traditions of her ancestors.
Now, when I attempt to recreate her soup, the result is merely adequate.
But the ritual itself sustains me — it is an intimate thread connecting past and present, a reminder that love often manifests in the simplest gestures.
